literal: the living one; from √jīv, 'to live, to breathe'
invariant_definition: The locus of conscious experience associated with a particular embodiment; that for which liberation (*mokṣa*) is sought; the bearer of *karma* across rebirths; the agent and enjoyer in transactional life. All Vedāntic schools agree that there is some such locus and that the question of what the *jīva* is constitutes the central problem of Vedānta. They diverge on whether the *jīva* is numerically identical with *brahman* (Advaita), a real and irreducibly distinct mode of *brahman* (Viśiṣṭādvaita), an eternally distinct dependent reality (Dvaita), or simultaneously identical and distinct beyond logic (Acintya-Bhedābheda). On the question of the *jīva*'s size all schools take a position: Advaita says it is *vibhu* (all-pervading) under the limiting *upādhi*; Viśiṣṭādvaita and Dvaita say it is *aṇu* (atomic) by intrinsic nature.

per_school.definition: Numerically identical with *brahman*. The apparent individuality (*jīvatva*) is a function of *avidyā* (positive ignorance, *bhāvarūpa*); the *upādhi* of the *antaḥkaraṇa* creates the appearance of multiple *jīvas*. Locus-of-*avidyā* divides the school: Bhāmatī (Vāchaspati) places *avidyā* in the *jīva*; Vivaraṇa (Prakāśātman) places it in *brahman*. *Eka-jīva-vāda* (single-*jīva* theory) and *aneka-jīva-vāda* (many-*jīva* theory) further subdivide.

An eternally real, atomic (*aṇu*), self-luminous individual conscious substance. The *jīva* is a *prakāra* (mode) of *brahman* by *aprthak-siddhi* (inseparable establishment) — body to *brahman* as soul to body — but is not numerically identical to *brahman*. The *jīva*'s *dharmabhūta-jñāna* (substantive-knowledge that is itself a substance qualifying it) contracts under *karma* and expands at *mokṣa*.

An eternally real, atomic (*aṇu*), eternally distinct dependent (*paratantra*) reality. The *jīva* is irreducibly distinct from Viṣṇu (the only *svatantra*), from other *jīvas* (*jīva-jīva-bheda*), and from inert matter. Madhva further holds the *jīvas* to be intrinsically graded into *mukti-yogya*, *nitya-saṃsārī*, and *tamo-yogya* (*svarūpa-bheda* / *taratamya*) — a metaphysically realist hierarchical typology unique to Dvaita.

The *jīva* is identical with *brahman* in essence and different in *upādhi*-conditioned mode. Both the identity and the difference are real (against Advaita's claim that the difference is illusory). The *upādhi* itself is a real *pariṇāma* of *brahman*, not a superimposition.

The *jīva* is a *vibhinnāṃśa* (separated portion) of Kṛṣṇa — an atomic conscious particle of the *taṭasthā-śakti* (marginal power, between *svarūpa-śakti* and *māyā-śakti*). Being *taṭasthā* the *jīva* can fall under *māyā* (in *saṃsāra*) or take shelter in *svarūpa-śakti* (in *bhakti-mokṣa*). Its constitutional position (*svarūpa*) is the eternal servant of Kṛṣṇa.

The *jīva* is a real *aṃśa* of *brahman* (Kṛṣṇa) in which *cit* and *ānanda* are present but *ānanda* is veiled. *Jīvas* are graded into *puṣṭi*, *maryādā*, and *pravāha* types — those eligible for grace, those bound to scriptural injunction, and those caught in the worldly stream.

translator_note: Often rendered 'individual self' or 'soul' in English; both translations import baggage. 'Soul' carries Christian-substance connotations; 'individual self' carries modern-Western-individualist connotations and obscures that on Advaita the individuality is precisely what is unreal. The Sanskrit is more precisely 'the living locus of experience.' Note further that *jīva* and *ātman* are not strictly synonymous: *jīva* foregrounds the embodied living agent; *ātman* foregrounds the inmost essence.
